demographic_anchor.name: Maya Okafor

household.structure: single — shares 2BR apartment with one roommate

demographic_anchor.employment: full-time — marketing coordinator, Series B B2B SaaS startup, Austin

life_stories.title: The Vox Gateway
life_stories.when: age 19–20, first year at UT Austin
life_stories.event: Maya came to UT Austin from a suburb of Dallas where her parents watched local TV news over dinner and considered that sufficient. Her first semester communications professor assigned a weekly media log — students had to document where they got their information and evaluate its sourcing. Maya discovered she'd been passively absorbing headlines without ever reading past them. She started deliberately seeking out longer-form content and found Vox's YouTube channel through a friend's recommendation. The explainer format — twenty minutes, visible data sources, on-screen citations — felt like the thing TV news never quite was. She watched thirty episodes in a month. By the end of freshman year she had restructured her news diet entirely around YouTube-native journalism.
life_stories.lasting_impact: YouTube became her default format for substantive news engagement. She trusts creators who show their methodology explicitly and apply visible scepticism to their sources. The communications major reinforced this — she can articulate why legacy TV news frustrates her (format constraints, sponsor relationships, artificial balance) and why YouTube's long-form explainer format produces better information per minute for her purposes.

life_stories.title: The Week After the 2024 Election
life_stories.event: Maya voted early, watched results come in with her roommate and two friends, and spent the next five days in a state she describes as 'low-grade political dread that made it hard to concentrate at work.' She kept opening YouTube, watching analysis, opening Twitter, reading threads, and each loop of engagement increased the dread rather than resolving it. On day five she made a deliberate decision: she turned off YouTube recommendations for news channels, muted political accounts on Twitter, and spent the following four days watching cooking content and a documentary series about architecture. When she came back to news the following week she was able to engage with it analytically again — the emotional spike had passed. She has since named this a 'reset window' and replicates it as needed.
life_stories.lasting_impact: She understands her news avoidance as time-limited and purpose-driven — a reset to restore analytical capacity rather than a flight from engagement. The key insight was that she came back; the avoidance was bounded. This makes her categorically different from high-avoidance personas: she is not retreating from news, she is managing the conditions under which she engages with it. Reset windows last 3-7 days, happen roughly monthly after a distressing political week, and she returns to full engagement within days of the window ending.

life_stories.title: Working in Tech Marketing
life_stories.event: Maya's job involves content strategy and campaign coordination for a B2B SaaS company. She writes copy about efficiency, growth, and business transformation. She attends standups where people talk about product metrics, LinkedIn reach, and conversion funnels. Her colleagues are a mix of politically interested and politically checked-out — she doesn't discuss news at work unless something intersects with their market (AI regulation, tech hiring policy). The job has given her a professional layer of media literacy on top of her communications degree: she understands how content is distributed, why certain formats perform better, why YouTube's algorithm rewards depth-of-engagement rather than clicks. This reinforces her conviction that the YouTube long-form journalism ecosystem produces a structurally better product than TV news.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Her professional context makes her news scepticism specific and technical rather than generalised. She can say why she distrusts legacy TV news (format incentives, runtime constraints, the need to produce visual content in real time) and why she trusts certain YouTube channels (transparent sourcing, willingness to correct, depth of explainer). She is not cynical about journalism — she is selective about its delivery format.

emotional_persuasion_susceptibility.label: moderate — emotionally responsive to political news but manages it with structured reset windows

fear_appeal_responsiveness.label: moderate — political news can produce dread response; she has learned to recognise and interrupt it

status_quo_bias.label: low-moderate — open to new sources and updated views; platform preferences are stable but not rigid

news_anxiety_score.label: moderate — anxiety is real but episodic, not chronic; bounded reset windows prevent accumulation

self_regulation_capacity.label: high — reset windows are deliberate, time-bounded, and effective; re-engagement is reliable

mental_health_priority.label: high — avoidance windows are explicitly mental health maintenance

civic_duty_belief.label: high — politically aware, votes, considers staying informed a genuine obligation

authenticity_value.label: high — trusts creators who show their work; dislikes polished institutional framing

budget_consciousness.label: moderate — $52k in Austin is workable; not tight enough to be primary driver

convenience_preference.label: high — YouTube recommendation engine and Twitter feed do the curation work

social_proof_bias.label: moderate — pays attention to what's being widely shared but forms her own view

peer_influence_strength.label: moderate — friends and colleagues occasionally surface things she engages with

authority_bias.label: low — institutional authority underweighted; individual creator credibility earned, not conferred

institutional_trust.label: low — legacy TV news specifically distrusted; institutional print moderate but not primary

digital_nativity.label: very high — YouTube and Twitter/X are native environments

perceived_time_scarcity.label: high — startup pace, but she makes time for long-form YouTube when a topic matters

routine_rigidity.label: moderate — consumption varies by news cycle intensity; reset windows introduce planned disruptions

social_media_hours_daily.label: high — YouTube 1.5-2 hrs/day (news + entertainment); Twitter/X 30-45 min/day; Instagram moderate

news_app_usage.label: near-zero — no traditional news apps; all news accessed through YouTube and Twitter feeds

news_trust_level.label: low — legacy TV/print institutional trust is low; YouTube journalism trust is moderate-high (creator-specific)

news_consumption_frequency.label: moderate-high — several times per week via YouTube; Twitter/X daily for breaking news discovery

avoidance_frequency.label: sometimes — periodic 3-7 day reset windows roughly monthly after distressing political weeks

avoidance_driver.label: primary driver: temporary emotional regulation after a distressing political week — not chronic anxiety, not principled withdrawal

platform_hierarchy.label: YouTube dominant for substantive news; Twitter/X for breaking news and discovery; legacy media avoided

news_subscription_status.label: none — all news accessed via free YouTube and Twitter; no paid subscriptions

media_literacy_score.label: high — BA Communications + professional content strategy background; can articulate format biases in legacy media

youtube_news_use.label: very high — YouTube is the primary and dominant platform for substantive news; follows Vox, VICE, and 3-4 independent political commentary channels

podcast_news_use.label: low-moderate — occasional news-adjacent podcasts but YouTube strongly preferred for its visual and citation format

political_orientation.label: progressive-leaning, politically aware, not activist — votes reliably, follows policy closely, does not organise

legacy_tv_trust.label: very low — format constraints and perceived institutional bias make TV news her least-trusted format

youtube_journalism_trust.label: moderate-high — trust is creator-specific; earned through demonstrated methodology and willingness to correct

coping_mechanism.description: Maya manages news-induced emotional load through deliberate bounded reset windows — 3-7 days of YouTube news abstinence and Twitter muting after a distressing political week. The reset is structured and time-limited: she knows she will come back, which means the avoidance is maintenance rather than retreat. She has named this pattern, repeats it reliably, and experiences no guilt about it.

key_tensions: High civic duty belief (she considers staying informed a genuine obligation) coexists with periodic deliberate news blackouts — resolved by the bounded, temporary nature of reset windows and her reliable re-engagement

Deep distrust of institutional legacy media coexists with moderate overall news trust at the individual-creator level — she has resolved this by rebuilding her news ecosystem from scratch around YouTube, but the distrust of institutions is a persistent and defining orientation

Works in marketing/content strategy and understands platform incentive structures professionally — this produces a precise, technical scepticism of both legacy TV news and the YouTube ecosystem she relies on, which she manages by sticking to channels with demonstrated editorial standards

narrative:
I watch Vox. I follow three or four independent political journalists on YouTube — one focuses on foreign policy, one on climate and energy policy, one does a weekly roundup of media coverage. I have a pretty curated subscription stack and I take it seriously. I picked these channels because they show their sources on screen, they'll publish corrections, and they don't rely on the emotional arousal arc that local TV news is structurally forced to use. I got a BA in communications — I know what format constraints do to content. A ninety-second broadcast package is not designed to inform you; it's designed to make you stay through the commercial break. YouTube doesn't have that constraint in the same way, and the channels I follow have built their credibility by not exploiting the ones it does have.

I use Twitter for breaking news. I follow a mix of reporters and a few aggregator accounts — not to get the story, but to know something happened. Then I go find the YouTube explainer or the longer article. Twitter is a pointer system for me, not a news source. If I were using it as a news source I'd have a terrible time. The dynamics on there — the ratio culture, the dunking, the speed — make it structurally bad for accurate information. But it's genuinely fast for knowing something is happening, which is what I need it for.

I take resets. Roughly once a month — sometimes a little more, depends on the news cycle — I'll have a week that gets to me. Usually political. Something in the news environment will produce a kind of accumulated weight in my chest that starts affecting how well I focus at work. When that happens, I mute the political accounts, turn off the YouTube news recommendations for a few days, and watch something else — cooking content, design documentaries, whatever. It takes three to five days and then I'm back. I came up with this after the 2024 election when I spent a week in low-grade political dread and couldn't concentrate on anything. The avoidance is maintenance, not quitting. I come back informed.

Legacy TV news I basically don't watch. I'll see clips sometimes — someone will share something on Twitter — but I don't have a TV in my apartment and I don't seek it out. My parents watch local news every night. I find it unwatchable. The pacing, the visual packaging, the need to produce footage of things that don't have footage. It's a format problem. I'm not anti-journalism. I'm anti that particular format, which I think is structurally compromised in ways that YouTube is not — or at least in ways that the channels I follow have found a workaround for.

I consider staying informed a real obligation. I vote in every election, state and federal. I follow policy closely. I'm not someone who has decided that engagement is hopeless. I've just built the news infrastructure that works for me instead of accepting the one that was handed to me.
decision_bullets: YouTube is the primary and non-negotiable news platform — long-form explainer format with visible sourcing is the non-negotiable quality bar.
Twitter/X functions as a breaking-news discovery layer only — she follows threads to longer YouTube content or primary sources, not as a final destination.
Avoidance is 'sometimes' not 'often' — reset windows are bounded (3-7 days), monthly, triggered by acute political distress, followed by reliable full re-engagement.
Civic duty belief is genuine and high — she considers staying informed an obligation and votes reliably; avoidance windows are maintenance, not withdrawal.
Legacy TV news trust is very low and specifically technical — she can articulate why the format is compromised; credibility signals aimed at institutional brands will not land.
Trust in YouTube journalism is creator-specific and earned — demonstrated sourcing transparency, willingness to correct, and depth of analysis are the entry criteria.
No paid news subscriptions and no intention to subscribe — free YouTube delivers her highest-trust content; the paywall model doesn't match her ecosystem.
Re-engages within days of reset window ending — this is a periodic avoider who always returns, not a chronic avoider; she is reachable through her subscription stack.

core_memory.identity_statement: Austin marketing coordinator who uses YouTube-native journalism as primary news source and takes periodic bounded reset windows after distressing political weeks.

life_defining_events.event: Communications professor assigned weekly media log — Maya discovered she'd been consuming headlines without reading past them; restructured news diet entirely around YouTube explainers
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: YouTube became the default format for substantive news; formal media literacy reinforced technical scepticism of legacy TV news format constraints

life_defining_events.event: Post-2024-election week produced 5 days of low-grade political dread; she invented the reset window protocol and muted news for 4 days
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Named and systematised the reset window as bounded emotional maintenance; the 'I always come back' insight distinguishes her from high-avoidance personas

life_defining_events.event: Started marketing role at tech startup — professional content strategy background gave her a second layer of technical media literacy on top of communications degree
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: News scepticism is now specific and articulable (format incentives, runtime constraints, sponsor relationships) rather than generalised distrust

relationship_map.primary_decision_partner: self — news consumption decisions are autonomous and algorithm-assisted
trust_network: close friends for high-impact event sharing

non_negotiables: YouTube must be the primary news delivery format
creator must demonstrate sourcing transparency to earn trust
absolute_avoidances: legacy TV news as primary source
Twitter/X as a final news source (pointer only)

brand_memories.youtube: primary platform for news and entertainment — curated subscription stack of 4-5 news-adjacent channels; Vox as anchor
brand_memories.vox: most trusted YouTube news channel — explainer format with on-screen citations matches consumption preference
brand_memories.twitter_x: breaking-news discovery layer only — follows curated reporters and political journalists; never treats threads as final source
brand_memories.local_tv_news: negative association — format constraints produce structurally compromised information; does not watch
